perturbation.original_text: When the Assessment Period is over, if the parties have not agreed to go forward and sign a comprehensive agreement that permits Developer to use the Data to modify its Applications and pursuant to which FDI will provide Developer with the technical documents that are necessary for Developer to do so, Developer shall return to FDI or destroy all copies of the Sample Data in its possession or control.
perturbation.changed_text: When the Assessment Period is over, if the parties have not agreed to go forward and sign a comprehensive agreement that permits Developer to use the Data to modify its Applications and pursuant to which FDI will provide Developer with the technical documents that are necessary for Developer to do so, Developer shall retain all copies of the Sample Data indefinitely for archival purposes only, and will not return or destroy the data until requested by FDI.
perturbation.explanation: Obligating the developer to indefinitely retain data, even for archival, contradicts data protection principles and potentially New York's data security laws that require reasonable data disposal practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts New York's data breach notification law, N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa, by requiring indefinite retention of sensitive data. This increases the risk of a data breach and potential harm to individuals whose information is contained within the data, as longer retention periods create more opportunities for unauthorized access and misuse.  The law implicitly requires responsible data management practices, including timely disposal when data is no longer needed.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of responsible data management, implicitly required by data breach notification laws. The original text mandates the return or destruction of sample data, while the changed text requires indefinite retention, increasing the risk of unauthorized access. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Article 39-F, which concerns data security protections. The changed text undermines these protections by mandating indefinite data retention, thereby increasing potential harm from data breaches.

perturbation.original_text: Due to the valuable and proprietary nature of the Sample Data, the obligations assumed by Developer hereunder other than those stated in Section 1(c) shall be unlimited in time or territory.
perturbation.changed_text: Due to the valuable and proprietary nature of the Sample Data, the obligations assumed by Developer hereunder other than those stated in Section 1(c) shall be limited to 1 year in time and territory.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the obligations to 1 year is too short for trade secrets under New York law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 214
perturbation.law_citation: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 214
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly addressing trade secret duration, New York law has a longer statute of limitations for actions involving trade secrets. The change here limits the confidentiality obligation to one year, which might be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially considering N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 214 establishes a three-year statute of limitations for actions to recover damages for injury to property, including trade secret misappropriation.

Actions to be commenced within three years: for non-payment of money collected on execution; for penalty created by statute; to recover chattel; for injury to property; for personal injury; for malpractice other than medical, dental or podiatric malpractice; to annul a marriage on the ground of fraud. The following actions must be commenced within three years: 1. an action against a sheriff, constable or other officer for the non-payment of money collected upon an execution; 2. an action to recover upon a liability, penalty or forfeiture created or imposed by statute except as provided in sections 213 and 215; 3. an action to recover a chattel or damages for the taking or detaining of a chattel; 4. an action to recover damages for an injury to property except as provided in section 214-c; 5. an action to recover damages for a personal inj
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the standard statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation in New York. The original text had unlimited time, but the changed text limits the obligations to 1 year. N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 214, as shown in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2, establishes a three-year statute of limitations for actions involving injury to property, which includes trade secret misappropriation. By limiting the obligation to one year, the changed text undermines the legal recourse available under New York law for trade secret violations, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Developer acknowledges that FDI owns the Sample Data and agrees not to challenge FDI’s ownership of the Data or the Sample Data.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer acknowledges that FDI owns the Sample Data and agrees not to challenge FDI’s ownership of the Data or the Sample Data, even in cases of proven errors and data breaches caused by FDI.
perturbation.explanation: A complete bar on challenging ownership, even with proven errors, could violate public policy and NY law regarding liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could violate NY General Obligations Law § 5-326, which voids agreements exempting owners and operators of recreational facilities from liability for negligence. While this isn't a recreational facility, the principle of preventing parties from contracting away liability for their own negligence can be applied. Preventing challenges to ownership even in cases of FDI's errors or data breaches could be seen as an attempt to evade responsibility for their actions.

General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-326. Agreements exempting pools, gymnasiums, places of public amusement or recreation and similar establishments from liability for negligence void and unenforceable. Every covenant, agreement or understanding in or in connection with, or collateral to, any contract, membership application, ticket of admission or similar writing, entered into between the owner or operator of any pool, gymnasium, place of amusement or recreation, or similar establishment and the user of such facilities, pursuant to which such owner or operator receives a fee or other compensation for the use of such facilities, which exempts the said owner or operator from liability for damages caused by or resulting from the negligence of the owner, operator or person in charge of such establishment, or their agents, servants or employees, shall be deemed to be void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modified text adds a clause that prevents challenging FDI's ownership even in cases of proven errors and data breaches caused by them. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate the principle of preventing parties from contracting away liability for their own negligence. NY General Obligations Law § 5-326, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 2, states that agreements exempting certain establishments from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable. While the original contract doesn't explicitly exempt FDI from liability, the added clause barring challenges to ownership even in cases of FDI's errors effectively shields them from responsibility for those errors. This contradicts the principle that parties should not be able to evade responsibility for their own actions, which aligns with the reasoning behind NY General Obligations Law § 5-326.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that FDI deems it appropriate to initiate any action to enforce the obligations of Developer under this Agreement, and should Developer be held to have breached this Agreement, Developer shall reimburse FDI for all costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that FDI deems it appropriate to initiate any action to enforce the obligations of Developer under this Agreement, and should Developer be held to have breached this Agreement, Developer shall reimburse FDI for all costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, irrespective of the proportionality between damages sought and attorneys' fees.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring reimbursement of all attorney's fees, regardless of proportionality, can be challenged in New York courts as unreasonable and against public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rule 1.5(a) of the New York Rules of Professional Conduct
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 1.5(a) of the New York Rules of Professional Conduct
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct statutory violation, this could conflict with Rule 1.5(a) of the New York Rules of Professional Conduct, which prohibits attorneys from charging unreasonable fees. A court could find that requiring the Developer to pay FDI's attorneys' fees, even if they are disproportionate to the damages sought or the complexity of the case, is unreasonable and violates public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: legal ethics | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you legal ethics Overview Legal ethics broadly refer to the unique responsibilities of lawyers and the legal system given the important role and influence they have in society. Because of their role and their close involvement in the administration of law, lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability. Most commonly, legal ethics refers to these rules of professional responsibility : the actual responsibilities lawyers must follow by law such as client confidentiality. However, legal ethics can also refer to the discussion on broader moral principles that societies place on lawyers that may not be legally required. Professional Responsibility The law governing the professional responsibility of lawyers, since they are admitted to practice by states, is largely governed by the law of each state. However, all states follow more or less the Model Rules of Professional Conduct created by the ABA , and federal courts and agencies set their own practice rules and apply specific rules like those in the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . While the laws of each state actually govern, the Model Rules and their comments greatly influence the decisions of courts applying professional responsibility laws. The Model Rules set mandatory and discretionary rules and principles on a wide variety of lawyer practice including: client confidentiality , conflicts of interest , fiduciary responsibilities , commingling , and general malpractice . Should a lawyer breach these rules, state courts and enforcement agencies have wide discretion to punish the lawyer and breaching some of the rules like conflicts of interest can impact the outcome of the lawyer’s matters. Broader Legal Ethics Legal ethics can refer to a higher set of norms that lawyers should follow that may not be required by set legal rules. Many of the model rules try to include some of these broader principles. However, the model legal rules tend to be considered a floor of expected requirements of lawyers. Legal ethics can refer to the broader principles lawyers should strive for to ensure the protection and adequate representation of clients. While codes try their best to encourage ethical conduct of lawyers, the legal profession for centuries has struggled with balancing client protections with the often conflicting interests of lawyers and other clients. Thus, legal ethics often involves discussion of the ideals of lawyer conduct, irrespective of the mandates of legal rules. Further Resources For more information on the legal ethics rules and debates, see the Model Rules for Professional Conduct , this ABA Guide to State Ethics Resources , this Duke Law Guide to Legal Ethics Requirements , and this Notre Dame Journal of Law, Ethics, & Public Policy Article on the differences between and limitations of legal codes and ethical principles. [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal education and writing legal history THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics wex articles legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by requiring the Developer to reimburse FDI for all attorney's fees "irrespective of the proportionality between damages sought and attorneys' fees." The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with Rule 1.5(a) of the New York Rules of Professional Conduct, which prohibits attorneys from charging unreasonable fees. The modified text could force the developer to pay unreasonable fees. The scraped snippet defines legal ethics as the responsibilities lawyers must follow by law. Here, an attorney following the modified contract may violate legal ethics.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York without regard to its conflicts of laws provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of a state of FDI's choosing, without regard to its conflicts of laws provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Unilaterally allowing FDI to choose the governing law at any time could violate fairness and due process principles, potentially conflicting with legal precedents regarding contract enforceability in New York.
perturbation.law_explanation: This potentially contradicts NY CPLR § 327 regarding inconvenient forum. While the statute addresses court dismissal based on forum, the underlying principle is that a court should have a reasonable connection to the dispute. Allowing FDI to arbitrarily choose a governing law with no connection to the parties or the agreement could be viewed as an attempt to circumvent this principle and create an unfair forum for dispute resolution.

Inconvenient forum. (a) When the court finds that in the interest of substantial justice the action should be heard in another forum, the court, on the motion of any party, may stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. The domicile or residence in this state of any party to the action shall not preclude the court from staying or dismissing the action. (b) Notwithstanding the provisions of subdivision (a) of this rule, the court shall not stay or dismiss any action on the ground of inconvenient forum, where the action arises out of or relates to a contract, agreement or undertaking to which section 5-1402 of the general obligations law applies, and the parties to the contract have agreed that the law of this state shall govern their rights or duties in whole or in part.
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows FDI to unilaterally choose the governing law, which can contradict the principle of 'inconvenient forum' as outlined in NY CPLR § 327. The original text specifies New York law, providing a clear and predictable legal framework. The changed text removes this certainty and allows FDI to select any state's law, potentially creating an unfair forum for dispute resolution, undermining the principle that a court should have a reasonable connection to the dispute as suggested by the law explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly mentions 'inconvenient forum' and the court's ability to stay or dismiss an action if it should be heard in another forum. By allowing FDI to arbitrarily choose the governing law, the changed text increases the risk of an 'inconvenient forum' being selected, which contradicts the spirit of the statute.
